title: Serotonin Syndrome
oneLiner: Potentially fatal drug-induced toxidrome from excessive CNS serotonin activity, characterised by clonus, hyperthermia and altered mental status – seen most often with drug interactions.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 28-year-old woman with depression on fluoxetine (SSRI) was started on linezolid for a VRE wound infection. On day 3 of linezolid, the nurse calls you because the patient is agitated, shivering and has a heart rate of 122 bpm. On exam, her temperature is 38.9°C, she has spontaneous clonus in both ankles and is hyperreflexic (3+ reflexes). She is sweaty, restless, and unable to sit still. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. She denies any rigidity.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues:**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| Young adult on *two* serotonergic agents (fluoxetine + linezolid) | Classic drug interaction – linezolid is a weak MAOI, adding to SSRI increases serotonin load |
| Onset rapidly after second agent added (3 days) | Serotonin syndrome develops within hours to days of a precipitating event (dose increase, new drug) |
| Agitation + shivering + tachycardia + hyperthermia | Autonomic instability – early sign of excess serotonin on hypothalamic thermoregulatory and autonomic centres |
| **Spontaneous clonus** | **Pathognomonic** – 5-HT₂A receptor overactivation in the spinal cord produces spontaneous, rhythmic contractions of ankle muscles |
| Hyperreflexia (3+), hyperactive bowel sounds | Diffuse serotonergic excess in the CNS and enteric nervous system |
| Rigidity absent | An important differentiator from neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), where rigidity is a core feature |

**Atypical presentations that might trick you:**

- **“Mild GI upset” variant:** Patient on SSRI + tramadol presents with only diarrhoea, nausea and mild anxiety. No clonus, no hyperthermia. You miss it – she progresses to clonus the next day after a second tramadol dose.
- **“Tremor only” in Parkinson’s disease:** A patient on selegiline (MAO-B) and levodopa adds an antidepressant (escitalopram). He develops bilateral leg tremor and confusion. Easily mistaken for worsening Parkinsonism – but look for clonus at the ankle, not rigidity.
- **In the ICU sedated patient:** A mechanically ventilated patient on fentanyl (weak serotonergic) receives a single dose of ondansetron for nausea. Nurses note frequent myoclonus, but sedation masks mental status changes – hyperthermia and hyperreflexia point you to serotonin syndrome.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Serotonin syndrome is a drug-induced toxidrome caused by excessive serotonin activity in the central nervous system and peripheral nerves. It exists on a spectrum from mild (tremor, diarrhoea) to life-threatening (rapid hyperthermia, seizures, coma). It is not an idiopathic or autoimmune disease – it is always iatrogenic.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** It is *not* rare – hundreds of cases reported each year, largely from medication interactions in outpatient psychiatry, perioperative settings, and HIV/infectious disease wards (linezolid use).
- **Stakes:** Untreated severe serotonin syndrome can cause rhabdomyolysis, DIC, multi-organ failure and death within hours.
- **Common pitfall:** It is frequently misdiagnosed as NMS, malignant hyperthermia, or sepsis because components of the triad (altered mental state, autonomic instability, temperature) overlap. The key pattern difference is **clonus** (serotonin syndrome) vs. **rigidity** (NMS). Missing this distinction leads to inappropriate treatments (e.g., dantrolene for “NMS” when actually serotonin syndrome) that may worsen the condition.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:**  
You take a drug that increases serotonin availability – either by blocking reuptake, increasing release, blocking metabolism (MAOI), or acting directly as a serotonergic agonist. Serotonin (5-HT) accumulates in the synaptic cleft and overstimulates postsynaptic receptors, particularly 5-HT₁A (mostly in the brainstem) and 5-HT₂A (spread throughout the CNS, spinal cord, and autonomic centres). The downstream effect is an uncontrolled “fight-or-flight” / parkinsonian-like dysregulation.

**Why each key clinical sign happens – mechanism → sign:**

- **5-HT₂A hyperactivation in the spinal cord → clonus and hyperreflexia.** The spinal cord CPG (central pattern generator) for locomotion becomes disinhibited, causing rhythmic contractions (clonus). Reflex arcs are facilitated.
- **5-HT₂A activation in the hypothalamus → hyperthermia.** The thermoregulatory set point is raised, but more importantly, there is uncontrolled heat production from muscle activity and uncoupled oxidative metabolism. This is why hyperthermia can become severe and refractory.
- **5-HT₁A and 5-HT₂A in the brainstem and limbic system → altered mental status.** Agitation, confusion, anxiety, and euphoria reflect direct serotonergic modulation of these regions.
- **Enteric 5-HT reaches massive excess → hyperactive bowel sounds, diarrhoea, vomiting.** The gut has 95% of total body serotonin; when drugs block its reuptake, intestinal peristalsis goes into overdrive.
- **5-HT₂A on vascular smooth muscle and cardiac muscle → autonomic instability (cyclical hypertension/hypotension, tachycardia, diaphoresis).**

**Connecting back to pattern recognition:**  
The vignette’s spontaneous clonus is the *direct clinical correlate* of spinal 5-HT₂A activation – not seen in NMS (which involves dopamine D2 blockade, causing rigidity instead). The absence of rigidity in our vignette (but presence of clonus) makes this serotonin syndrome, not NMS.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|---|---|---|
| **MAOIs (irreversible & reversible)** | Phenelzine, tranylcypromine, isocarboxazid, linezolid, selegiline (MAO-B), methylene blue IV, St John’s Wort (MAOI activity) | “Recently started on antidepressant + antibiotic for VRE” (linezolid) or “Patient took St John’s Wort with their SSRI” |
| **SSRIs / SNRIs** | Fluoxetine, paroxetine, citalopram, venlafaxine, duloxetine | Most common single-agent cause – but usually only when combined with another serotonergic |
| **TCAs** | Clomipramine, amitriptyline | Especially clomipramine (itself has some 5-HT uptake block) plus any other serotonergic |
| **Opioids** | Tramadol, fentanyl, meperidine (pethidine), methadone (weak), tapentadol | “Post-op patient given tramadol after epidural fentanyl – now myoclonic” |
| **Antiemetics (some)** | Ondansetron, metoclopramide, granisetron | Usually low risk alone, but can add when patient already on SSRI |
| **Other drugs** | MDMA (ecstasy), LSD, buspirone, lithium, 5-HTP (OTC supplement), dextromethorphan, valproate (weak) | “Party drug” or “supplements” history |
| **Drug interactions (most dangerous)** | MAOI + SSRI, MAOI + TCA, MAOI + meperidine, SSRI + linezolid, MAOI + St John’s Wort | *Overwhelmingly the most common cause of severe serotonin syndrome* |

**Most common cause overall:** Combination of an MAOI (including linezolid or methylene blue) with an SSRI or other 5-HT reuptake inhibitor.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
The **Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria** (1998) is the clinical gold standard. It has greater sensitivity (84%) and specificity (97%) than the older Sternbach criteria. Diagnosis is made if the patient has taken a serotonergic agent **AND** any ONE of the following:

- Spontaneous clonus
- Inducible clonus + agitation or diaphoresis
- Ocular clonus + agitation or diaphoresis
- Tremor + hyperreflexia
- Hypertonia + temperature >38°C + ocular or inducible clonus

**Key takeaway:** If you see **spontaneous clonus**, you have confirmed serotonin syndrome (in the right drug context). That single finding *confirms* the diagnosis.

**What tests to order and why:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Serum CK** | May be elevated (mild) from muscle activity | To rule out rhabdomyolysis in severe cases / differentiate from NMS | CK > 1000 U/L more typical of NMS with rigidity; in SS, usually < 500 unless severe hyperthermia and prolonged myoclonus |
| **Serum electrolytes, renal, LFTs** | Assess for end-organ damage / DIC | Hyperthermia can cause AKI, transaminitis, coagulopathy | Mild elevation common; severe derangements signal life-threatening toxicity |
| **ECG** | Tachycardia, QT interval (watch for drugs like citalopram or methadone that also prolong QT) | Rule out arrhythmia, check QTc at baseline | QTc >500 ms is a separate risk; not specific to SS but important if giving drugs like cyproheptadine |
| **Drug screen / Tylenol level** | May reveal use of MDMA, dextromethorphan, or hidden serotonergic | In undifferentiated agitated patient, screen for ingestions | Positive for any serotonergic agent supports the diagnosis, but never rules it in alone |
| **Cerebral imaging / LP** | Nonspecific | Only if meningitis, SAH, or structural lesion is in differential (e.g., altered mental state + fever) | Normal imaging supports toxidrome; do not routinely order unless atypical features (e.g., focal neuro signs) |

**Single finding that confirms:** **Spontaneous clonus** in a patient on a serotonergic agent.  
**Single finding that rules out:** **Severe rigidity without any clonus** is much more NMS or malignant hyperthermia.

**What NOT to do:** Order a “serotonin level” – it is not measured clinically and has no role in diagnosis. The diagnosis is purely clinical.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
#### Immediate (first hour)

- **Stop all serotonergic agents** – this is non-negotiable. Document the drugs implicated.
- **IV benzodiazepines** – first-line for agitation, hyperreflexia, and autonomic instability.
    - Diazepam 5–10 mg IV over 2 minutes, repeat every 10 minutes as needed, or
    - Lorazepam 2–4 mg IV, same interval.
    *Why:* Benzodiazepines enhance GABA-ergic inhibition, which dampens serotonergic overdrive and reduces muscle activity, lowering temperature and protecting against rhabdomyolysis.
- **Aggressive cooling** if temperature >39°C – tepid sponging, cool IV fluids, cooling blanket. Avoid alcohol rubs.
    *Why:* Hyperthermia is the immediate life threat – can lead to DIC and multi-organ failure quickly.
- **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline 500–1000 mL bolus if hypotensive or dehydrated.
- **Antidote (if moderate-severe):** **Cyproheptadine** – a 5-HT₂A antagonist.
    - Dose: 12 mg PO/NG initially, then 2 mg every 2 hours as needed up to 32 mg/day.
    - *Why:* Blocks the receptor most responsible for clonus and autonomic signs.
    - *Pitfall:* Cyproheptadine has some antihistaminergic effect – don’t mistake its sedation for worsening mental status. It is **not** available IV, so if the patient is severely agitated with NGT, you may need to administer per NG.
- **Avoid** propranolol or other beta-blockers – they can cause unopposed alpha-adrenergic activity, worsening hypertension and tachyarrhythmias, and can induce hypotension.
- **Avoid** bromocriptine – used for NMS, but in serotonin syndrome can worsen central serotonin excess (it is a D2 agonist but can also have serotonergic activity).

#### Ward (24–72 h)

- Continue supportive care – benzodiazepines tapered over 24 hours.
- Repeat cyproheptadine 2 mg every 4–6 hours PRN for persistent clonus or agitation (max 32 mg/day).
- Monitor for **rhabdomyolysis** – check CK every 6 hours if CK > 500 at baseline.
- **Observe in a monitored bed** – cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia, pulse oximetry for respiratory depression.
- **Reintroduction of serotonergic agents:** Do not restart the offending combination. Educate patient and document in notes. Future antidepressant choices should avoid overlapping mechanisms.

#### Long-term

- Refer to psychiatry or clinical pharmacology for safe antidepressant optimisation.
- If the serotonin syndrome was precipitated by an MAOI (e.g., phenelzine), the patient needs a 2-week washout before starting a different class (4 weeks if switching from MAOI to an SSRI).
- For linezolid-induced cases: alternative antibiotics are needed for the remaining treatment duration.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Serotonin Syndrome | Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome | Malignant Hyperthermia | Anticholinergic Syndrome | Thyroid Storm |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|---------------|
| **Onset** | Rapid (hours to 1–2 days) | Subacute (days to weeks on antipsychotic) | Rapid (minutes to hours after inhalational anaesthetic / succinylcholine) | Rapid (hours after anticholinergic overdose) | Subacute (days; precipitant: infection, thyroxine overdose) |
| **Drug history** | SSRI, MAOI, linezolid, tramadol, etc. | Typical antipsychotic (haloperidol) or recent dose increase | Inhalational anaesthetic (sevoflurane, halothane) +/- succinylcholine | Anticholinergic (diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, tricyclic overdose) | Thyroxine, amiodarone, lithium |
| **Neuromuscular** | Clonus (spontaneous), hyperreflexia, tremor | Rigidity (lead-pipe), bradykinesia, no clonus | Rigidity (generalised), masseter spasm, hypertonicity | Hyperreflexia? No. More tremor, myoclonus less common; may have ataxia | Tremor, but no clonus; hyperreflexia |
| **Mental status** | Agitation, anxiety, confusion | Stupor, mutism, catatonia | Agitation then coma | Delirium, psychosis, picking at sheets | Agitation, psychosis |
| **Autonomic** | Hyperthermia, tachycardia, diaphoresis, hypertension/hypotension | Hyperthermia, tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis | Hyperthermia (rapid and severe), tachycardia, tachypnoea, cyanosis | Hyperthermia, tachycardia, dry skin, flushed, urinary retention | Hyperthermia, tachycardia, palpitations, diarrhoea, exophthalmos |
| **Bowel sounds** | Hyperactive | Normal | Normal | Decreased (ileus) | Normal or increased |
| **Pupils** | Mydriasis | Normal | Normal | Mydriasis (dilated) | Normal |
| **CK** | Usually normal or mildly elevated (< 500) | Elevated (> 1000, often > 5000) | Markedly elevated | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiating feature** | Spontaneous clonus | Absence of clonus; rigidity, bradykinesia, high CK | History of anaesthesia + rigidity + hypercapnia + acute rise in end-tidal CO2 + CK | Dry skin, dilated pupils, urinary retention, hypoactive bowel sounds | Hot, sweaty, exophthalmos, goitre, high thyroid hormones (low TSH) |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
If not recognised and treated emergently, serotonin syndrome can progress rapidly.

**Major complication: Severe hyperthermia (>41°C)**
- *How it presents:* Unresponsive, rigid (though still may have clonus), seizures, coma. Temperature continues to rise despite external cooling.
- *Key signs:* Core temperature 41°C+, metabolic acidosis, elevated lactate, rising CK, coagulopathy (DIC).
- *What to do:* This is a **critical care emergency**. Activate the ICU team. Intubate and ventilate. Use **propofol** for sedation (also reduces cerebral metabolism). **Ice water lavage** (nasogastric, bladder, rectal) if external cooling fails. **Dantrolene** is *not* the first choice (it is for malignant hyperthermia), but some literature suggests it may help if there is severe rigidity and hyperthermia refractory to benzodiazepines. However, first-line sedation with midazolam and paralysis (rocuronium) will stop muscle activity enough to lower temperature.

**Late-presentation pattern (the exam stem):**
- “A 52-year-old man on phenelzine who accidentally took a friend’s escitalopram 3 days ago now presents with confusion, fever, and rigidity. BP 80/50, HR 130, temperature 40.5°C, GCS 9, with ankle clonus and hyperreflexia. He has not passed urine for 12 hours.”
- This patient has **severe serotonin syndrome with complications**: rhabdomyolysis → AKI, hyperthermia → DIC, possible compartment syndrome from rigidity. Immediate steps: stop drugs, IV benzodiazepines, aggressive cooling, ICU referral, haemodialysis may be needed for myoglobinuria-induced AKI.

**Other complications:**
- **Rhabdomyolysis:** from sustained myoclonus/rigidity leading to AKI. Check CK, monitor urine output, IV fluids at 200 mL/h until CK < 5000 or falling.
- **DIC:** from hyperthermia causing platelet consumption and coagulopathy. Check platelets, PT/APTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer. Treat with supportive care (cooling, platelets if bleeding).
- **Seizures:** from severe metabolic derangement. Treat with benzodiazepines first; avoid phenytoin.
- **Respiratory failure:** from excessive muscle activity leading to lactic acidosis and eventually exhaustion/aspiration. Intubate early.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: SEROTONIN**

- **S** – Seizures
- **E** – Elevated temperature (hyperthermia)
- **R** – Rigidity (actually less specific; **clonus** is better – so some use “C” instead)
- **O** – Ocular clonus
- **T** – Tremor
- **O** – Overactive bowel sounds (diarrhoea)
- **N** – Neuromuscular (hyperreflexia, myoclonus)
- **I** – Increased autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis)
- **N** – Not NMS (remember: **C**lonus = serotonin, **R**igidity = NMS)

**Better mnemonic: “Hunter’s criteria”** – just remember the single pathognomonic sign: **Spontaneous clonus + serotonergic agent = serotonin syndrome.**

**5–7 High-yield pearls:**

1. **Clonus is king.** If you see spontaneous clonus in any patient on any serotonergic drug, stop the drugs and treat. No test needed.

2. **Not just SSRIs.** Linezolid (antibiotic), methylene blue (IV dye), tramadol, fentanyl, dextromethorphan, St John’s Wort, and even MDMA are all common culprits.

3. **Withdrawing the offending drug is often enough for mild cases** (discontinuing the SSRI may cause serotonin withdrawal, but treat the acute syndrome first – the withdrawal can be managed later).

4. **Benzodiazepines are first-line therapy**, not only for sedation but to reduce muscle activity and heat generation. They are highly effective.

5. **Do not use dantrolene** unless you are certain the diagnosis is malignant hyperthermia or malignant hyperthermia is co-present (rare). Dantrolene has no proven benefit in serotonin syndrome.

6. **Never give a MAOI + SSRI within 2–4 weeks of each other** (washout period). This is a prescription error that still happens.

7. **A patient on an SSRI undergoing surgery may be given fentanyl, ondansetron, or linezolid** – the risk of serotonin syndrome is real. Ask anaesthetists to use propofol and avoid serotonergic adjuncts where possible.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Mistaking serotonin syndrome for sepsis because of fever and tachycardia – look for clonus and bowel sounds.
- Starting dantrolene before confirming NMS – wastes time and can confuse the picture.
- Using propranolol to control tachycardia – worsens hypertension due to unopposed alpha stimulation.
- Giving bromocriptine or amantadine (as for NMS) – these can increase serotonin synthesis or release.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Trigger | Action |
|---|---|
| **Temperature > 40°C** (NEWS2 respiratory rate? – hyperthermia itself) | **Call ICU** immediately – likely to require intubation, paralysis, aggressive cooling |
| **GCS < 8** or inability to protect airway | **Call anaesthesia / ICU** for intubation |
| **Seizures** (new onset) | **Crash team** – treat with benzodiazepines; if persists, call ICU |
| **Sustained HR > 140 bpm** despite fluid and sedation | **ICU** – may need vasoactive support (but avoid beta-blockers; consider esmolol in extreme cases under ICU guidance) |
| **Systolic BP < 90 mmHg** despite fluid | **ICU** – may need norepinephrine |
| **CK > 5,000 U/L** or rising rapidly | **Renal / ICU** – risk acute kidney injury from myoglobinuria |
| **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** | **ICU** – sign of hypoperfusion or severe metabolic acidosis from muscle activity |
| **Respiratory rate > 30** or SpO2 < 92% on room air | **Call medical review** – could be developing respiratory failure |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 7** | **Urgent senior review** (usually medical registrar) |
| **Any sign of DIC** (purpura, oozing, abnormal clotting) | **ICU / haematology** – supportive care, treat hyperthermia |

**Final note:** Early escalation saves

rawMarkdown:
## Serotonin Syndrome

**One-liner:** Potentially fatal drug-induced toxidrome from excessive CNS serotonin activity, characterised by clonus, hyperthermia and altered mental status – seen most often with drug interactions.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 28-year-old woman with depression on fluoxetine (SSRI) was started on linezolid for a VRE wound infection. On day 3 of linezolid, the nurse calls you because the patient is agitated, shivering and has a heart rate of 122 bpm. On exam, her temperature is 38.9°C, she has spontaneous clonus in both ankles and is hyperreflexic (3+ reflexes). She is sweaty, restless, and unable to sit still. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. She denies any rigidity.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues:**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| Young adult on *two* serotonergic agents (fluoxetine + linezolid) | Classic drug interaction – linezolid is a weak MAOI, adding to SSRI increases serotonin load |
| Onset rapidly after second agent added (3 days) | Serotonin syndrome develops within hours to days of a precipitating event (dose increase, new drug) |
| Agitation + shivering + tachycardia + hyperthermia | Autonomic instability – early sign of excess serotonin on hypothalamic thermoregulatory and autonomic centres |
| **Spontaneous clonus** | **Pathognomonic** – 5-HT₂A receptor overactivation in the spinal cord produces spontaneous, rhythmic contractions of ankle muscles |
| Hyperreflexia (3+), hyperactive bowel sounds | Diffuse serotonergic excess in the CNS and enteric nervous system |
| Rigidity absent | An important differentiator from neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), where rigidity is a core feature |

**Atypical presentations that might trick you:**

- **“Mild GI upset” variant:** Patient on SSRI + tramadol presents with only diarrhoea, nausea and mild anxiety. No clonus, no hyperthermia. You miss it – she progresses to clonus the next day after a second tramadol dose.
- **“Tremor only” in Parkinson’s disease:** A patient on selegiline (MAO-B) and levodopa adds an antidepressant (escitalopram). He develops bilateral leg tremor and confusion. Easily mistaken for worsening Parkinsonism – but look for clonus at the ankle, not rigidity.
- **In the ICU sedated patient:** A mechanically ventilated patient on fentanyl (weak serotonergic) receives a single dose of ondansetron for nausea. Nurses note frequent myoclonus, but sedation masks mental status changes – hyperthermia and hyperreflexia point you to serotonin syndrome.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Serotonin syndrome is a drug-induced toxidrome caused by excessive serotonin activity in the central nervous system and peripheral nerves. It exists on a spectrum from mild (tremor, diarrhoea) to life-threatening (rapid hyperthermia, seizures, coma). It is not an idiopathic or autoimmune disease – it is always iatrogenic.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** It is *not* rare – hundreds of cases reported each year, largely from medication interactions in outpatient psychiatry, perioperative settings, and HIV/infectious disease wards (linezolid use).
- **Stakes:** Untreated severe serotonin syndrome can cause rhabdomyolysis, DIC, multi-organ failure and death within hours.
- **Common pitfall:** It is frequently misdiagnosed as NMS, malignant hyperthermia, or sepsis because components of the triad (altered mental state, autonomic instability, temperature) overlap. The key pattern difference is **clonus** (serotonin syndrome) vs. **rigidity** (NMS). Missing this distinction leads to inappropriate treatments (e.g., dantrolene for “NMS” when actually serotonin syndrome) that may worsen the condition.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:**  
You take a drug that increases serotonin availability – either by blocking reuptake, increasing release, blocking metabolism (MAOI), or acting directly as a serotonergic agonist. Serotonin (5-HT) accumulates in the synaptic cleft and overstimulates postsynaptic receptors, particularly 5-HT₁A (mostly in the brainstem) and 5-HT₂A (spread throughout the CNS, spinal cord, and autonomic centres). The downstream effect is an uncontrolled “fight-or-flight” / parkinsonian-like dysregulation.

**Why each key clinical sign happens – mechanism → sign:**

- **5-HT₂A hyperactivation in the spinal cord → clonus and hyperreflexia.** The spinal cord CPG (central pattern generator) for locomotion becomes disinhibited, causing rhythmic contractions (clonus). Reflex arcs are facilitated.
- **5-HT₂A activation in the hypothalamus → hyperthermia.** The thermoregulatory set point is raised, but more importantly, there is uncontrolled heat production from muscle activity and uncoupled oxidative metabolism. This is why hyperthermia can become severe and refractory.
- **5-HT₁A and 5-HT₂A in the brainstem and limbic system → altered mental status.** Agitation, confusion, anxiety, and euphoria reflect direct serotonergic modulation of these regions.
- **Enteric 5-HT reaches massive excess → hyperactive bowel sounds, diarrhoea, vomiting.** The gut has 95% of total body serotonin; when drugs block its reuptake, intestinal peristalsis goes into overdrive.
- **5-HT₂A on vascular smooth muscle and cardiac muscle → autonomic instability (cyclical hypertension/hypotension, tachycardia, diaphoresis).**

**Connecting back to pattern recognition:**  
The vignette’s spontaneous clonus is the *direct clinical correlate* of spinal 5-HT₂A activation – not seen in NMS (which involves dopamine D2 blockade, causing rigidity instead). The absence of rigidity in our vignette (but presence of clonus) makes this serotonin syndrome, not NMS.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|---|---|---|
| **MAOIs (irreversible & reversible)** | Phenelzine, tranylcypromine, isocarboxazid, linezolid, selegiline (MAO-B), methylene blue IV, St John’s Wort (MAOI activity) | “Recently started on antidepressant + antibiotic for VRE” (linezolid) or “Patient took St John’s Wort with their SSRI” |
| **SSRIs / SNRIs** | Fluoxetine, paroxetine, citalopram, venlafaxine, duloxetine | Most common single-agent cause – but usually only when combined with another serotonergic |
| **TCAs** | Clomipramine, amitriptyline | Especially clomipramine (itself has some 5-HT uptake block) plus any other serotonergic |
| **Opioids** | Tramadol, fentanyl, meperidine (pethidine), methadone (weak), tapentadol | “Post-op patient given tramadol after epidural fentanyl – now myoclonic” |
| **Antiemetics (some)** | Ondansetron, metoclopramide, granisetron | Usually low risk alone, but can add when patient already on SSRI |
| **Other drugs** | MDMA (ecstasy), LSD, buspirone, lithium, 5-HTP (OTC supplement), dextromethorphan, valproate (weak) | “Party drug” or “supplements” history |
| **Drug interactions (most dangerous)** | MAOI + SSRI, MAOI + TCA, MAOI + meperidine, SSRI + linezolid, MAOI + St John’s Wort | *Overwhelmingly the most common cause of severe serotonin syndrome* |

**Most common cause overall:** Combination of an MAOI (including linezolid or methylene blue) with an SSRI or other 5-HT reuptake inhibitor.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**
The **Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria** (1998) is the clinical gold standard. It has greater sensitivity (84%) and specificity (97%) than the older Sternbach criteria. Diagnosis is made if the patient has taken a serotonergic agent **AND** any ONE of the following:

- Spontaneous clonus

**What tests to order and why:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Serum CK** | May be elevated (mild) from muscle activity | To rule out rhabdomyolysis in severe cases / differentiate from NMS | CK > 1000 U/L more typical of NMS with rigidity; in SS, usually < 500 unless severe hyperthermia and prolonged myoclonus |
| **Serum electrolytes, renal, LFTs** | Assess for end-organ damage / DIC | Hyperthermia can cause AKI, transaminitis, coagulopathy | Mild elevation common; severe derangements signal life-threatening toxicity |
| **ECG** | Tachycardia, QT interval (watch for drugs like citalopram or methadone that also prolong QT) | Rule out arrhythmia, check QTc at baseline | QTc >500 ms is a separate risk; not specific to SS but important if giving drugs like cyproheptadine |
| **Drug screen / Tylenol level** | May reveal use of MDMA, dextromethorphan, or hidden serotonergic | In undifferentiated agitated patient, screen for ingestions | Positive for any serotonergic agent supports the diagnosis, but never rules it in alone |
| **Cerebral imaging / LP** | Nonspecific | Only if meningitis, SAH, or structural lesion is in differential (e.g., altered mental state + fever) | Normal imaging supports toxidrome; do not routinely order unless atypical features (e.g., focal neuro signs) |

**What NOT to do:** Order a “serotonin level” – it is not measured clinically and has no role in diagnosis. The diagnosis is purely clinical.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

#### Immediate (first hour)

- **Stop all serotonergic agents** – this is non-negotiable. Document the drugs implicated.
- **IV benzodiazepines** – first-line for agitation, hyperreflexia, and autonomic instability.
    - Diazepam 5–10 mg IV over 2 minutes, repeat every 10 minutes as needed, or
    - Lorazepam 2–4 mg IV, same interval.
    *Why:* Benzodiazepines enhance GABA-ergic inhibition, which dampens serotonergic overdrive and reduces muscle activity, lowering temperature and protecting against rhabdomyolysis.
- **Aggressive cooling** if temperature >39°C – tepid sponging, cool IV fluids, cooling blanket. Avoid alcohol rubs.
    *Why:* Hyperthermia is the immediate life threat – can lead to DIC and multi-organ failure quickly.
- **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline 500–1000 mL bolus if hypotensive or dehydrated.
- **Antidote (if moderate-severe):** **Cyproheptadine** – a 5-HT₂A antagonist.
    - Dose: 12 mg PO/NG initially, then 2 mg every 2 hours as needed up to 32 mg/day.
    - *Why:* Blocks the receptor most responsible for clonus and autonomic signs.
    - *Pitfall:* Cyproheptadine has some antihistaminergic effect – don’t mistake its sedation for worsening mental status. It is **not** available IV, so if the patient is severely agitated with NGT, you may need to administer per NG.
- **Avoid** propranolol or other beta-blockers – they can cause unopposed alpha-adrenergic activity, worsening hypertension and tachyarrhythmias, and can induce hypotension.
- **Avoid** bromocriptine – used for NMS, but in serotonin syndrome can worsen central serotonin excess (it is a D2 agonist but can also have serotonergic activity).

#### Ward (24–72 h)

- Continue supportive care – benzodiazepines tapered over 24 hours.
- Repeat cyproheptadine 2 mg every 4–6 hours PRN for persistent clonus or agitation (max 32 mg/day).
- Monitor for **rhabdomyolysis** – check CK every 6 hours if CK > 500 at baseline.
- **Observe in a monitored bed** – cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia, pulse oximetry for respiratory depression.
- **Reintroduction of serotonergic agents:** Do not restart the offending combination. Educate patient and document in notes. Future antidepressant choices should avoid overlapping mechanisms.

#### Long-term

- Refer to psychiatry or clinical pharmacology for safe antidepressant optimisation.
- If the serotonin syndrome was precipitated by an MAOI (e.g., phenelzine), the patient needs a 2-week washout before starting a different class (4 weeks if switching from MAOI to an SSRI).
- For linezolid-induced cases: alternative antibiotics are needed for the remaining treatment duration.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Serotonin Syndrome | Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome | Malignant Hyperthermia | Anticholinergic Syndrome | Thyroid Storm |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|---------------|
| **Onset** | Rapid (hours to 1–2 days) | Subacute (days to weeks on antipsychotic) | Rapid (minutes to hours after inhalational anaesthetic / succinylcholine) | Rapid (hours after anticholinergic overdose) | Subacute (days; precipitant: infection, thyroxine overdose) |
| **Drug history** | SSRI, MAOI, linezolid, tramadol, etc. | Typical antipsychotic (haloperidol) or recent dose increase | Inhalational anaesthetic (sevoflurane, halothane) +/- succinylcholine | Anticholinergic (diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, tricyclic overdose) | Thyroxine, amiodarone, lithium |
| **Neuromuscular** | Clonus (spontaneous), hyperreflexia, tremor | Rigidity (lead-pipe), bradykinesia, no clonus | Rigidity (generalised), masseter spasm, hypertonicity | Hyperreflexia? No. More tremor, myoclonus less common; may have ataxia | Tremor, but no clonus; hyperreflexia |
| **Mental status** | Agitation, anxiety, confusion | Stupor, mutism, catatonia | Agitation then coma | Delirium, psychosis, picking at sheets | Agitation, psychosis |
| **Autonomic** | Hyperthermia, tachycardia, diaphoresis, hypertension/hypotension | Hyperthermia, tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis | Hyperthermia (rapid and severe), tachycardia, tachypnoea, cyanosis | Hyperthermia, tachycardia, dry skin, flushed, urinary retention | Hyperthermia, tachycardia, palpitations, diarrhoea, exophthalmos |
| **Bowel sounds** | Hyperactive | Normal | Normal | Decreased (ileus) | Normal or increased |
| **Pupils** | Mydriasis | Normal | Normal | Mydriasis (dilated) | Normal |
| **CK** | Usually normal or mildly elevated (< 500) | Elevated (> 1000, often > 5000) | Markedly elevated | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiating feature** | Spontaneous clonus | Absence of clonus; rigidity, bradykinesia, high CK | History of anaesthesia + rigidity + hypercapnia + acute rise in end-tidal CO2 + CK | Dry skin, dilated pupils, urinary retention, hypoactive bowel sounds | Hot, sweaty, exophthalmos, goitre, high thyroid hormones (low TSH) |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

If not recognised and treated emergently, serotonin syndrome can progress rapidly.

**Major complication: Severe hyperthermia (>41°C)**
- *How it presents:* Unresponsive, rigid (though still may have clonus), seizures, coma. Temperature continues to rise despite external cooling.
- *Key signs:* Core temperature 41°C+, metabolic acidosis, elevated lactate, rising CK, coagulopathy (DIC).
- *What to do:* This is a **critical care emergency**. Activate the ICU team. Intubate and ventilate. Use **propofol** for sedation (also reduces cerebral metabolism). **Ice water lavage** (nasogastric, bladder, rectal) if external cooling fails. **Dantrolene** is *not* the first choice (it is for malignant hyperthermia), but some literature suggests it may help if there is severe rigidity and hyperthermia refractory to benzodiazepines. However, first-line sedation with midazolam and paralysis (rocuronium) will stop muscle activity enough to lower temperature.

**Late-presentation pattern (the exam stem):**
- “A 52-year-old man on phenelzine who accidentally took a friend’s escitalopram 3 days ago now presents with confusion, fever, and rigidity. BP 80/50, HR 130, temperature 40.5°C, GCS 9, with ankle clonus and hyperreflexia. He has not passed urine for 12 hours.”
- This patient has **severe serotonin syndrome with complications**: rhabdomyolysis → AKI, hyperthermia → DIC, possible compartment syndrome from rigidity. Immediate steps: stop drugs, IV benzodiazepines, aggressive cooling, ICU referral, haemodialysis may be needed for myoglobinuria-induced AKI.

**Other complications:**
- **Rhabdomyolysis:** from sustained myoclonus/rigidity leading to AKI. Check CK, monitor urine output, IV fluids at 200 mL/h until CK < 5000 or falling.
- **DIC:** from hyperthermia causing platelet consumption and coagulopathy. Check platelets, PT/APTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer. Treat with supportive care (cooling, platelets if bleeding).
- **Seizures:** from severe metabolic derangement. Treat with benzodiazepines first; avoid phenytoin.
- **Respiratory failure:** from excessive muscle activity leading to lactic acidosis and eventually exhaustion/aspiration. Intubate early.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: SEROTONIN**

- **S** – Seizures
- **E** – Elevated temperature (hyperthermia)
- **R** – Rigidity (actually less specific; **clonus** is better – so some use “C” instead)
- **O** – Ocular clonus
- **T** – Tremor
- **O** – Overactive bowel sounds (diarrhoea)
- **N** – Neuromuscular (hyperreflexia, myoclonus)
- **I** – Increased autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis)
- **N** – Not NMS (remember: **C**lonus = serotonin, **R**igidity = NMS)

**Better mnemonic: “Hunter’s criteria”** – just remember the single pathognomonic sign: **Spontaneous clonus + serotonergic agent = serotonin syndrome.**

**5–7 High-yield pearls:**

1. **Clonus is king.** If you see spontaneous clonus in any patient on any serotonergic drug, stop the drugs and treat. No test needed.

2. **Not just SSRIs.** Linezolid (antibiotic), methylene blue (IV dye), tramadol, fentanyl, dextromethorphan, St John’s Wort, and even MDMA are all common culprits.

3. **Withdrawing the offending drug is often enough for mild cases** (discontinuing the SSRI may cause serotonin withdrawal, but treat the acute syndrome first – the withdrawal can be managed later).

4. **Benzodiazepines are first-line therapy**, not only for sedation but to reduce muscle activity and heat generation. They are highly effective.

5. **Do not use dantrolene** unless you are certain the diagnosis is malignant hyperthermia or malignant hyperthermia is co-present (rare). Dantrolene has no proven benefit in serotonin syndrome.

6. **Never give a MAOI + SSRI within 2–4 weeks of each other** (washout period). This is a prescription error that still happens.

7. **A patient on an SSRI undergoing surgery may be given fentanyl, ondansetron, or linezolid** – the risk of serotonin syndrome is real. Ask anaesthetists to use propofol and avoid serotonergic adjuncts where possible.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Mistaking serotonin syndrome for sepsis because of fever and tachycardia – look for clonus and bowel sounds.
- Starting dantrolene before confirming NMS – wastes time and can confuse the picture.
- Using propranolol to control tachycardia – worsens hypertension due to unopposed alpha stimulation.
- Giving bromocriptine or amantadine (as for NMS) – these can increase serotonin synthesis or release.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Trigger | Action |
|---|---|
| **Temperature > 40°C** (NEWS2 respiratory rate? – hyperthermia itself) | **Call ICU** immediately – likely to require intubation, paralysis, aggressive cooling |
| **GCS < 8** or inability to protect airway | **Call anaesthesia / ICU** for intubation |
| **Seizures** (new onset) | **Crash team** – treat with benzodiazepines; if persists, call ICU |
| **Sustained HR > 140 bpm** despite fluid and sedation | **ICU** – may need vasoactive support (but avoid beta-blockers; consider esmolol in extreme cases under ICU guidance) |
| **Systolic BP < 90 mmHg** despite fluid | **ICU** – may need norepinephrine |
| **CK > 5,000 U/L** or rising rapidly | **Renal / ICU** – risk acute kidney injury from myoglobinuria |
| **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** | **ICU** – sign of hypoperfusion or severe metabolic acidosis from muscle activity |
| **Respiratory rate > 30** or SpO2 < 92% on room air | **Call medical review** – could be developing respiratory failure |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 7** | **Urgent senior review** (usually medical registrar) |
| **Any sign of DIC** (purpura, oozing, abnormal clotting) | **ICU / haematology** – supportive care, treat hyperthermia |

**Final note:** Early escalation saves
